description: This figure presents low-cycle fatigue cyclic response curves showing stress amplitude versus number of cycles for Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels. The graph compares two distinct alloy systems: Fe-30Mn-9Al-1C (dotted curves, reference [161]) and Fe-30Mn-10Al-0.4C (solid curves, reference [162]). For the Fe-30Mn-9Al-1C austenitic alloy, three aging conditions are shown—550°C/24 h, 710°C/2 h, and 550°C/2 h—all tested at a fixed total strain amplitude of 0.8%. These curves exhibit pronounced cyclic softening behavior, with the 550°C/24 h aged specimen showing the highest initial stress amplitude (approximately 1050 MPa decaying to 750 MPa), attributed to planar slip, shearing of j-carbides, and precipitate dissolution. For the duplex Fe-30Mn-10Al-0.4C alloy (65% austenite + 35% ferrite), six total strain amplitudes are presented ranging from 0.2% to 2.0%. At low strain amplitudes (0.2–0.4%), the material shows cyclic softening; at intermediate amplitudes (0.8–1.0%), it exhibits initial cyclic hardening followed by softening; while at high amplitudes (1.5–2.0%), rapid hardening occurs before failure. The figure demonstrates that aging treatments significantly influence the cyclic stability of austenitic Fe-30Mn-9Al-1C, while the duplex Fe-30Mn-10Al-0.4C displays strain-amplitude-dependent cyclic deformation mechanisms, with fatigue crack initiation occurring in the ferrite phase.
